Content:
Hi all, there has been a bug around with Vietnamese keyboard using Telex in MacOS for a long time. For example, to type "ở", you have to type "o" then "w" then "r". It's called Telex. On typing "w" (with Vietnamese keyboard on, of course), the letter "o" disappears or you only get "ow" or nothing or other unexpected ways. When you press backspace, letters before it just added, e.g. "o ngngngng". I had to turn the Vietnamese keyboard on and off several times (switching to another langugage) to get it work normally.
This mainly happens with non-Apple apps. I think this is a serious internal macOS error. So please someone who can contact Apple developers, report this issue to them. Thanks.

194: const State = extern struct {
195:     // The effective buffer to process Vietnamese input. we will skip processing if the buffer
196:     // longer than 16.
197:     buffer_effective: [16]Span,
198:     // The maximum buffer length that the engine still keeps the effective buffer, after the maximum
199:     // value (255), we will reset the effective buffer and this value.
200:     buffer_length: u8 = 0,
201:     // Mark the earliest position (inclusive) in buffer where we modified the span, will be used to
202:     // calculate backspaces and replacement characters.
203:     buffer_modification_index: i8 = -1,
204:     // Mark the position (inclusive) in buffer where we will switch to literal mode (due to manually
205:     // switch, input cancellation, exceed effective buffer). This value is independent from mode and
206:     // has higher priority, e.g. if the mode is `telex` but the engine is working on position on or
207:     // after literal index, the engine will skip Vietnamese input processing (we only count positive
208:     // value, -1 mean no literal index).
209:     literal_index: i8 = -1,
210:     // Determine if we will process input in the specified mode (Telex) or append the character as is.
211:     mode: InputMode = .literal,
212: 
213:     // Initialize the State on allocated memory.
214:     pub fn init(state: *State) void {
215:         state.* = .{
216:             .buffer_effective = undefined,
217:         };
218:     }
219: };
220: 
221: // Simple ABI wrapper for initialize allocated memory.
222: export fn lex_state_init(state: *State) void {
223:     State.init(state);
224: }
225: 
226: // Needed for the caller to allocate memory for our State.
227: export const lex_state_size: usize = @sizeOf(State);
228: 
229: // Needed for the caller to allocate memory for our State.
230: export const lex_state_alignment: usize = @alignOf(State);
231: 
232: // Add operations to the given state based on Telex rules, operations are determined by the input
233: // character.
234: export fn lex_add(state: *State, c: u8) void {
235:     // Only allow a-zA-Z.
236:     assert(std.ascii.isAlphabetic(c));
237: 
238:     // The buffer_modification index must be inbound of the buffer_effective.
239:     assert(state.buffer_modification_index >= -1 and state.buffer_modification_index < state.buffer_effective.len);
240: 
241:     // The literal_index value must be in range -1 -> 16 (buffer_effective length) because we won't
242:     // process Vietnamese input outside the buffer_effective.
243:     assert(state.literal_index >= -1 and state.literal_index <= state.buffer_effective.len);
244: 
245:     // Input mode must be either .literal or .telex.
246:     assert(state.mode == .literal or state.mode == .telex);
247: 
248:     switch (c) {
249:         'A', 'a' => { // circumflex.
250:             if (state.literal_index > -1 or state.mode == .literal) {
251:                 // Enable literal input when literal_index is set or on literal mode.
252: 
253:                 // Check if we can add new span for input character.
254:                 if (state.buffer_length < state.buffer_effective.len) {
255:                     // Add character to span.
256:                     state.buffer_effective[state.buffer_length] = Span.init(c);
257:                 }
258:                 // Increase the buffer length for tracking, we will need it went handling backspace.
259:                 state.buffer_length = state.buffer_length + 1;
260: 
261:                 // Set modification index to -1 because we didn't modify any existing span.
262:                 state.buffer_modification_index = -1;
263:             } else {
264:                 // Vietnamese input.
265:                 // Possible cases:
266:                 // - circumplex.
267:                 // - cancel circumflex.
268:                 // - literal.
269:             }
270:         },
271:         'C', 'c' => {}, // fill missing diacritic, e.g. cước.
272:         'D', 'd' => {}, // bar.
273:         'E', 'e' => {}, // circumflex.
274:         'F', 'f' => {}, // falling.
275:         'I', 'i' => {}, // fill missing diacritic, e.g. người.
276:         'J', 'j' => {}, // falling_glottalized.
277:         'M', 'm' => {}, // fill missing diacritic, e.g. cườm.
278:         'N', 'n' => {}, // fill missing diacritic, e.g. cường.
279:         'O', 'o' => {}, // circumflex.
280:         'P', 'p' => {}, // fill missing diacritic, e.g. cướp.
281:         'R', 'r' => {}, // dipping_rising.
282:         'S', 's' => {}, // rising.
283:         'T', 't' => {}, // fill missing diacritic, e.g. trượt.
284:         'U', 'u' => {}, // fill missing diacritic, e.g. hươu.
285:         'W', 'w' => {}, // breve.
286:         'X', 'x' => {}, // rising_glottalized.
287:         'Z', 'z' => {}, // level / reset.
288:         else => { // literal.
289:             // These characters will be added to state literally.
290: 
291:             // Check if we can add new span for input character.
292:             if (state.buffer_length < state.buffer_effective.len) {
293:                 // Add character to span.
294:                 state.buffer_effective[state.buffer_length] = Span.init(c);
295:             }
296:             // Increase the buffer length for tracking, we will need it went handling backspace.
297:             state.buffer_length = state.buffer_length + 1;
298: 
299:             // Set modification index to -1 because we didn't modify any existing span.
300:             state.buffer_modification_index = -1;
301:             // Set literal index if it's not set and the length exceed the buffer_effective.
302:             if (state.literal_index == -1 and state.buffer_length >= state.buffer_effective.len) {
303:                 // Set literal mode since buffer index 16 (just after the last buffer_effective
304:                 // item) because we don't have span for these position, we stop processing Telex
305:                 // rules but we will continue processing if user use backspaces to move back to the
306:                 // buffer_effective range, all existing spans must work as expected.
307:                 state.literal_index = state.buffer_effective.len;
308:             }
309:         },
310:     }
311: }

Under Telex, the branch is not just “circumflex / cancel circumflex / literal”.

## Missing Cases

- Plain append in Telex mode: first `a`, or any `a` that does not have an immediately eligible target, should append a plain `a` while staying in Telex mode. That is different from full literal bypass.
- Apply circumflex to a plain trailing `a/A`: `aa -> â`, case-insensitive in recognition, but preserving the target span’s case.
- Apply circumflex to an already toned `a/A`: if the current last active span is `á`, `à`, `ả`, `ã`, or `ạ`, another `a` should produce the circumflex form with the same tone, not drop the tone.
- Cancel a previously applied circumflex: if the last active span is `â/Â`, another `a` should recover literal text. The standard Telex recovery rule is “repeat the second key to get distinct characters”, so this is the `aaa -> aa` family of behavior.
- Cancel a toned circumflex: if the last active span is `ấ/ầ/ẩ/ẫ/ậ`, recovery must also define what happens to the tone. The prototype chooses to clear tone on the modified span, then append a plain `a`.
- Cross-modifier refusal: if the last active span is `ă/Ă`, pressing `a` should not silently replace breve with circumflex. It should append plain `a`.
- Non-local fallback: if the nearest preceding `a` is not the immediate active tail, `a` should not scan backward through the word to find some older `a` to modify.
- Custom onset gate: the repo explicitly wants words beginning with non-Vietnamese onset letters `f`, `j`, `w`, `z` to stay unaccented, so `a` must be blocked from `aa -> â` inside those words too, not just handled locally. That requirement is stated in the main [README](file:///<redacted>/lex/README.md#L22-L24).
- Literal-boundary gate: the state comments define `literal_index` as a position boundary, not a global permanent flag, so the branch conceptually needs “current position is at/after literal boundary” as a case distinct from “some literal index exists somewhere”. The current condition in [the branch](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/liblex.zig#L249-L263) is broader than that design comment suggests.

## Approach

The clean approach is:

- Treat `a` as a local doubled-vowel modifier, like the prototype does in its [doubled-vowel block](file:///<redacted>/lex/playground/prototype-app/src/liblex.zig#L446-L465).
- Use word-level context only as a gate, not as a target finder. The gate is for things like the custom non-Vietnamese onset rule and literal-boundary behavior.
- Keep target selection immediate-tail only. For `a`, that means:
  - if the last active span is plain `a/A`, apply circumflex;
  - if the last active span is already circumflexed `a/A`, cancel into literal recovery;
  - otherwise append plain `a/A`.
- Preserve tone when applying circumflex to an already toned `a`.
- Do not let `a` replace a different diacritic already on `a`, especially breve. `aw` belongs to the `w` rule, so the `a` rule should respect that separation.
- Make state transitions explicit, because this branch has three different kinds of outcomes:
  - modify existing span only;
  - modify existing span and append one new plain `a`;
  - append one plain `a` with no modification.
- Define case handling explicitly: recognition should be case-insensitive, but output should preserve the target span’s case for modification and the pressed key’s case for any appended recovery character.

One deeper design issue sits underneath this: if `buffer_length` is meant to track the effective visible spans, then `aa -> â` is a modify-only case and should not grow visible length. If it is meant to track raw keypress count, the current state shape is missing a separate effective-span length. The comments around [`State`](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/liblex.zig#L194-L218) read more like effective-buffer semantics than raw-key history, so this branch should be designed around visible-span updates.

## Oracle Perspective

Oracle agreed with the main direction and pushed one point hard: for `a`, broader word-level search is the wrong model.

- Use broader context only to decide whether Vietnamese processing is allowed at all.
- Do not search backward through the word for an older `a` to mutate.
- Keep `aa` strictly local to the immediate last active `a`, because that matches standard Telex better and avoids surprising retroactive edits.
- The main risks are:
  - forgetting the repo’s onset-disable rule;
  - letting `a` reach across a literal boundary;
  - silently replacing `ă` with `â`;
  - leaving mixed-case behavior undefined;
  - not testing repeated sequences like `aaa` and `aaaa`.

The prototype architecture description in [the prototype README](file:///<redacted>/lex/playground/prototype-app/README.md#L3-L10) is consistent with that: doubled-vowel modifiers are local, while broader syllable analysis is reserved for tone placement and other word-level features.

No code changed. Prototype uses a Carbon global hot key, not the event tap, to handle Ctrl+Opt+Space.

## Prototype approach

In [prototype `Lex.swift`](file://<redacted>/lex/playground/prototype-app/macos/Lex.swift#L1-L14):

- Imports `Carbon`.
- Defines a stable `EventHotKeyID`.
- Stores `hotKeyRef: EventHotKeyRef?` on `AppDelegate`.

In [prototype registration](file://<redacted>/lex/playground/prototype-app/macos/Lex.swift#L105-L128):

- Installs a Carbon keyboard event handler with `InstallEventHandler`.
- Extracts the pressed hot key ID from the Carbon event.
- If it matches `toggleHotKeyID`, calls `toggleVietnamese()`.
- Registers the shortcut with:

```swift
RegisterEventHotKey(
    UInt32(kVK_Space),
    UInt32(controlKey | optionKey),
    id,
    GetApplicationEventTarget(),
    0,
    &hotKeyRef
)
```

So Ctrl+Opt+Space is handled globally by Carbon as `kVK_Space + controlKey | optionKey`.

In [prototype toggle behavior](file://<redacted>/lex/playground/prototype-app/macos/Lex.swift#L130-L139), it:

- Flips `vietnameseEnabled`.
- Resets the engine.
- Updates the menu bar title.
- Plays a feedback sound.

## Current app mapping

The current app already has the right UI state shape:

- `AppState.mode` is the source of truth for menu icon selection in [current `Lex.swift`](file://<redacted>/lex/macos/Lex.swift#L8-L15).
- `InputMode` already has `.literal` and `.telex` in [current `Lex.swift`](file://<redacted>/lex/macos/Lex.swift#L72-L79).
- `AppDelegate` owns the engine state lifecycle in [current `Lex.swift`](file://<redacted>/lex/macos/Lex.swift#L19-L59).

The main gap: current Swift can toggle `AppState.mode`, but the C ABI only exposes init/add/backspace in [src/lex.h](file://<redacted>/lex/src/lex.h#L17-L25). There is no exported function yet to set the Zig engine mode or reset it explicitly.

## Proposed plan

1. **Add Carbon hot key support to `macos/Lex.swift`.**
   - Add `import Carbon`.
   - Add a file-private `toggleHotKeyID`.
   - Add `private var hotKeyRef: EventHotKeyRef?` to `AppDelegate`.

2. **Register Ctrl+Opt+Space during app launch.**
   - Call `registerHotKey()` from `applicationDidFinishLaunching` after `initializeEngineState()`.
   - Use the prototype’s Carbon pattern:
     - `InstallEventHandler`
     - `GetEventParameter`
     - `RegisterEventHotKey(UInt32(kVK_Space), UInt32(controlKey | optionKey), ...)`

3. **Add a current-app toggle method.**
   - Add something like `toggleInputMode()` on `AppDelegate`.
   - It should run on the main actor and flip:

   ```swift
   appState.mode = appState.mode == .telex ? .literal : .telex
   ```

   The existing `MenuBarExtra` icon should update automatically because it reads `appDelegate.appState.mode`.

4. **Dispose of the hot key on termination.**
   - In `applicationWillTerminate`, call `UnregisterEventHotKey` if `hotKeyRef` is set.
   - Then continue existing cleanup: destroy engine state and remove signal sources.

5. **Engine integration decision.**
   - For a UI-only shortcut, only `macos/Lex.swift` needs to change.
   - For a real input-mode switch, I would also add a small Zig/C ABI:
     - `lex_set_mode(void *state, uint8_t mode)` or equivalent.
     - Update `src/lex.h` because the project instruction requires rewriting the C header when the C ABI changes.
   - Then `toggleInputMode()` would update both `AppState.mode` and the engine state.

Recommended scope: implement the Carbon hot key and Swift `AppState.mode` toggle first in `macos/Lex.swift`; add the Zig ABI only if the shortcut must immediately affect engine processing, not just current app UI state.

`toggleHotKeyID` is the Carbon identifier used to distinguish this global hot key from any other hot keys the app might register.

In the prototype:

```swift
private let toggleHotKeyID = EventHotKeyID(signature: OSType(0x4C455821), id: 1) // "LEX!"
```

## What `EventHotKeyID` does

Carbon hot keys are registered globally with `RegisterEventHotKey`. When the shortcut is pressed, Carbon sends a `kEventHotKeyPressed` event. The event handler receives only a generic keyboard event, so the app needs to extract the `EventHotKeyID` from the event and compare it with the ID it registered.

Flow:

```diagram
╭────────────────────╮
│ Ctrl + Opt + Space │
╰─────────┬──────────╯
          ▼
╭────────────────────╮
│ Carbon hot key     │
│ system dispatch    │
╰─────────┬──────────╯
          ▼
╭────────────────────╮
│ App event handler  │
│ receives event     │
╰─────────┬──────────╯
          ▼
╭────────────────────╮
│ Extract            │
│ EventHotKeyID      │
╰─────────┬──────────╯
          ▼
╭────────────────────╮
│ Compare with       │
│ toggleHotKeyID     │
╰─────────┬──────────╯
          ▼
╭────────────────────╮
│ toggleInputMode()  │
╰────────────────────╯
```

The `EventHotKeyID` has two parts:

```swift
EventHotKeyID(
    signature: OSType(...),
    id: ...
)
```

### `signature`

The signature is an `OSType`, historically a four-character code packed into a `UInt32`.

Prototype value:

```swift
OSType(0x4C455821)
```

That hex encodes:

```text
0x4C = L
0x45 = E
0x58 = X
0x21 = !
```

So the signature is `"LEX!"`.

Purpose:

- Namespaces the hot key for this app.
- Makes the ID easier to identify/debug.
- Avoids relying only on a bare integer like `1`.

I would keep the same value in the current app:

```swift
private let toggleHotKeyID = EventHotKeyID(
    signature: OSType(0x4C455821),
    id: 1
)
```

### `id`

The `id` is the app-local hot key number.

Prototype uses:

```swift
id: 1
```

That is enough if the app only has one global hot key. If later we add more shortcuts, we can keep the same signature and use more IDs:

```swift
private let toggleHotKeyID = EventHotKeyID(signature: OSType(0x4C455821), id: 1)
private let lockInputHotKeyID = EventHotKeyID(signature: OSType(0x4C455821), id: 2)
```

Then the event handler can switch on `hotKeyID.id`.

## Why not detect Ctrl+Opt+Space in the event tap?

The prototype does not use the event tap for this shortcut. It uses Carbon global hot-key registration.

That is the better fit here because:

- It is explicitly for global shortcuts.
- It works independently from the text-processing event tap.
- It avoids mixing “mode switch command” with “input character processing”.
- Ctrl+Opt+Space should not be treated like normal text input anyway.
- Carbon will handle the key combination as a registered command and call the app handler.

## Current-app implementation shape

The current app would add:

```swift
import Carbon
```

Near the top:

```swift
private let toggleHotKeyID = EventHotKeyID(
    signature: OSType(0x4C455821),
    id: 1
)
```

In `AppDelegate`:

```swift
private var hotKeyRef: EventHotKeyRef?
private var toggleSound: NSSound?
```

Then during launch:

```swift
func applicationDidFinishLaunching(_ notification: Notification) {
    print("Activated.")
    installSignalHandler(SIGINT)
    installSignalHandler(SIGTERM)
    initializeEngineState()
    registerHotKey()
    // Start event tap.
}
```

And during termination:

```swift
func applicationWillTerminate(_ notification: Notification) {
    print("Shutting down...")
    // Stop event tap.
    unregisterHotKey()
    destroyEngineState()
    signalSources.removeAll()
}
```

## Registration details

The registration method would be adapted from the prototype:

```swift
private func registerHotKey() {
    let selfPtr = Unmanaged.passUnretained(self).toOpaque()

    var eventType = EventTypeSpec(
        eventClass: OSType(kEventClassKeyboard),
        eventKind: UInt32(kEventHotKeyPressed)
    )

    InstallEventHandler(
        GetApplicationEventTarget(),
        { _, event, userData -> OSStatus in
            guard let userData else {
                return OSStatus(eventNotHandledErr)
            }

            var hotKeyID = EventHotKeyID()

            GetEventParameter(
                event!,
                EventParamName(kEventParamDirectObject),
                EventParamType(typeEventHotKeyID),
                nil,
                MemoryLayout<EventHotKeyID>.size,
                nil,
                &hotKeyID
            )

            if hotKeyID.signature == toggleHotKeyID.signature &&
                hotKeyID.id == toggleHotKeyID.id {
                let appDelegate = Unmanaged<AppDelegate>
                    .fromOpaque(userData)
                    .takeUnretainedValue()

                Task { @MainActor in
                    appDelegate.toggleInputMode()
                }
            }

            return noErr
        },
        1,
        &eventType,
        selfPtr,
        nil
    )

    var id = toggleHotKeyID

    RegisterEventHotKey(
        UInt32(kVK_Space),
        UInt32(controlKey | optionKey),
        id,
        GetApplicationEventTarget(),
        0,
        &hotKeyRef
    )
}
```

I would compare both `signature` and `id`, not only `id`. The prototype only checks `id`, which is fine for one hot key, but checking both is safer and still simple.

## Unregistration

The current app should clean up the registration:

```swift
private func unregisterHotKey() {
    guard let hotKeyRef else {
        return
    }

    UnregisterEventHotKey(hotKeyRef)
    self.hotKeyRef = nil
}
```

This is not in the prototype, but it is appropriate for the current app’s existing cleanup style.

## Audio handling

The prototype does this:

```swift
toggleSound?.stop()
toggleSound = NSSound(named: NSSound.Name(vietnameseEnabled ? "Tink" : "Pop"))
toggleSound?.play()
```

That means:

- Stop any previous sound if it is still playing.
- Pick a different built-in macOS sound depending on the new mode.
- Keep the `NSSound` instance in a property so it is not immediately deallocated before playback.

For the current app, I would mirror that idea:

```swift
private var toggleSound: NSSound?
```

Then in the toggle method:

```swift
@MainActor
private func toggleInputMode() {
    appState.mode = appState.mode == .telex ? .literal : .telex

    // Optional: reset engine or set engine mode here.
    // Current app does not yet expose a C ABI for setting Zig mode.

    playToggleSound()
}
```

With:

```swift
@MainActor
private func playToggleSound() {
    toggleSound?.stop()

    let soundName: NSSound.Name = appState.mode == .telex ? "Tink" : "Pop"
    toggleSound = NSSound(named: soundName)
    toggleSound?.play()
}
```

Suggested mapping:

| New mode | Sound | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| `.telex` | `"Tink"` | input processing enabled |
| `.literal` | `"Pop"` | literal mode enabled |

This matches the prototype’s intent:

```swift
vietnameseEnabled ? "Tink" : "Pop"
```

Current app equivalent:

```swift
appState.mode == .telex ? "Tink" : "Pop"
```

## Updated plan with audio included

1. Add `import Carbon` to `macos/Lex.swift`.
2. Add `toggleHotKeyID` as a file-private constant.
3. Add `hotKeyRef` and `toggleSound` properties to `AppDelegate`.
4. Register Ctrl+Opt+Space during launch with `RegisterEventHotKey`.
5. In the Carbon handler, compare the received `EventHotKeyID` against `toggleHotKeyID`.
6. On match, call `toggleInputMode()` on the main actor.
7. `toggleInputMode()` flips `appState.mode`.
8. `toggleInputMode()` calls `playToggleSound()`.
9. `playToggleSound()` stops the previous sound, chooses `"Tink"` for `.telex` and `"Pop"` for `.literal`, stores it in `toggleSound`, then plays it.
10. Unregister the hot key during app termination.

One design decision remains: whether the shortcut should only update Swift UI state now, or also update the Zig engine mode. If it must update the engine, I would add a small C ABI such as `lex_state_set_mode`, and update both `src/lex.zig` and `src/lex.h`.

## Menubar icon switching

This line already encodes the icon switch:

```swift
MenuBarExtra(
    "Lex",
    systemImage: appDelegate.appState.mode == .telex
        ? "character.textbox.badge.sparkles"
        : "textformat.size.smaller"
) {

}
```

Because `AppState.mode` is `@Published`, toggling this:

```swift
appState.mode = appState.mode == .telex ? .literal : .telex
```

should cause SwiftUI to re-render the `MenuBarExtra` label and swap the SF Symbol.

Current mapping:

| Mode | Icon |
|---|---|
| `.telex` | `character.textbox.badge.sparkles` |
| `.literal` | `textformat.size.smaller` |

That is a good current-app equivalent of the prototype’s title switch:

```swift
button.title = vietnameseEnabled ? "Ꝟ" : "𝙰"
```

Prototype uses direct AppKit status item mutation; current app should prefer SwiftUI state-driven rendering, which you are already doing.

## Audio mapping issue

Your current sound mapping is inverted compared with the previous proposal and the prototype’s semantics:

```swift
let soundName: NSSound.Name = appState.mode == .telex ? "Pop" : "Tink"
```

Prototype does:

```swift
vietnameseEnabled ? "Tink" : "Pop"
```

If `.telex` means Vietnamese/Telex processing enabled, then prototype-equivalent mapping is:

```swift
let soundName: NSSound.Name = appState.mode == .telex ? "Tink" : "Pop"
```

Recommended meaning:

| New mode after toggle | Sound |
|---|---|
| `.telex` | `"Tink"` |
| `.literal` | `"Pop"` |

If you intentionally want `"Pop"` to mean “switch activated into Telex”, then your version is fine. But if the goal is to copy prototype behavior, swap it.

## Hot key ID details in your code

This is good:

```swift
// Namespace: LEX!
private let toggleInputModeHotKeyId = EventHotKeyID(signature: OSType(0x4C455821), id: 1)
```

`0x4C455821` is the four-character code `LEX!`:

```text
4C = L
45 = E
58 = X
21 = !
```

Your handler also improves on the prototype by comparing both fields:

```swift
if hotKeyId.signature == toggleInputModeHotKeyId.signature &&
        hotKeyId.id == toggleInputModeHotKeyId.id {
    ...
}
```

That is better than comparing only `id`.

One naming note: Swift convention usually spells initialisms consistently. Since the type is `EventHotKeyID`, `toggleInputModeHotKeyID` would be more idiomatic than `toggleInputModeHotKeyId`. Not required, but it matches Apple naming.

## Current toggle flow

Your current flow is:

```diagram
╭────────────────────╮
│ Ctrl + Opt + Space │
╰─────────┬──────────╯
          ▼
╭────────────────────╮
│ Carbon handler     │
│ receives hot key   │
╰─────────┬──────────╯
          ▼
╭────────────────────╮
│ toggleInputMode()  │
│ on MainActor       │
╰─────────┬──────────╯
          ▼
╭────────────────────╮
│ appState.mode flip │
╰─────────┬──────────╯
          ▼
╭────────────────────╮
│ SwiftUI menubar    │
│ icon re-renders    │
╰─────────┬──────────╯
          ▼
╭────────────────────╮
│ play mode sound    │
╰────────────────────╯
```

That is the right shape for the current SwiftUI menu bar app.

## Suggested refinements before implementation continues

I would adjust only these details:

1. **Fix or confirm audio direction.**

   Prototype-equivalent:


2. **Consider extracting audio into a helper for readability.**

   ```swift

   private func toggleInputMode() {
       appState.mode = appState.mode == .telex ? .literal : .telex
       // TODO: this flow must change when finish integrating with engine.
       playToggleInputModeSound()
   }

   @MainActor
   private func playToggleInputModeSound() {
       toggleInputModeSound?.stop()
       let soundName: NSSound.Name = appState.mode == .telex ? "Tink" : "Pop"
       toggleInputModeSound = NSSound(named: soundName)
       toggleInputModeSound?.play()
   }
   ```

3. **Check registration result.**

   `RegisterEventHotKey` returns an `OSStatus`. Current code ignores it. Better:

   ```swift
   let status = RegisterEventHotKey(...)
   if status != noErr {
       print("Failed to register toggle input mode hot key: \\(status)")
   }
   ```

   Same for `InstallEventHandler` if you want clearer diagnostics.

4. **Dispose the event handler if stored.**

   Right now you unregister the hot key, which is the important part. `InstallEventHandler` can also return an `EventHandlerRef` if you pass a pointer instead of `nil`. Not necessary for one app-lifetime handler, but if being strict, store and remove it too.

5. **Menubar menu item should reflect current mode.**


   - make a non-private method intended for UI actions, or
   - keep hot-key handling private and add a separate menu action method.

   Example shape:

   ```swift

   func switchInputMode() {

   }

   @MainActor
   private func toggleInputMode() {
       ...
   }
   ```

## Important access-control issue

`toggleInputMode()` is currently `private`, so `LexMenu` cannot call it. That is fine if only the hot key toggles mode.

But the existing menu has:

```swift
Button("Lock inputs") {}
```

If this button is meant to switch mode too, the delegate needs a callable public/internal method. For example:

```swift

func toggleInputModeFromUserAction() {
    toggleInputMode()
}
```

Then:

```swift

    appDelegate.toggleInputModeFromUserAction()

```

1. Keep `MenuBarExtra(systemImage:)` as the icon-switch source of truth.
2. Change sound mapping to `.telex ? "Tink" : "Pop"` unless you intentionally want the reverse.
3. Add a menu item that mirrors the shortcut and shows the target mode.
4. Make the menu item call the same toggle path as the hot key.
5. Optionally check/log Carbon registration failures.
6. Later, when engine integration is ready, update `toggleInputMode()` to also set/reset Zig engine mode through a C ABI.

`AppState` is observable, but `LexApp` is not directly observing it as a SwiftUI dynamic property. `LexMenu` observes it, so the menu contents can refresh, but the `MenuBarExtra` scene label may not be invalidated when `appDelegate.appState.mode` changes.

## Likely cause

This is the important distinction:

```swift
@NSApplicationDelegateAdaptor(AppDelegate.self)
private var appDelegate
```

SwiftUI owns the app delegate, but it does not automatically subscribe to nested observable objects accessed through the delegate:

```swift
appDelegate.appState.mode
```

So this read may happen when the scene is created, but not re-run when `mode` changes.

The menu content has:

```swift
@ObservedObject var appState: AppState
```

So `LexMenu` observes `AppState`.

But the menubar label itself is not in `LexMenu`; it is in the `MenuBarExtra` scene declaration.

## Smallest fix to try

Use the label-closure overload of `MenuBarExtra`, and put the observing logic into a small SwiftUI `View`.

Instead of:

```swift
MenuBarExtra("Lex", systemImage: appDelegate.appState.mode == .telex ? "character.textbox.badge.sparkles" : "textformat.size.smaller") {
    LexMenu(
        appDelegate: appDelegate,
        appState: appDelegate.appState
    )
}
.menuBarExtraStyle(.menu)
```

Use:

```swift
MenuBarExtra {
    LexMenu(
        appDelegate: appDelegate,
        appState: appDelegate.appState
    )
} label: {
    LexMenuBarLabel(appState: appDelegate.appState)
}
.menuBarExtraStyle(.menu)
```

Then add:

```swift
struct LexMenuBarLabel: View {
    @ObservedObject var appState: AppState

    var body: some View {
        Label("Lex", systemImage: appState.mode.menuBarSystemImage)
    }
}
```

And put the icon mapping on `InputMode`:

```swift
enum InputMode {
    case literal, telex

    var menuBarSystemImage: String {
        switch self {
        case .telex:
            "character.textbox.badge.sparkles"
        case .literal:
            "textformat.size.smaller"
        }
    }
}
```

This makes the menubar label itself observe `AppState`.

## Why this is better than the current expression

Current:

```swift
systemImage: appDelegate.appState.mode == .telex ? ... : ...
```

That is just a value passed into a scene initializer.

Proposed:

```swift
LexMenuBarLabel(appState: appDelegate.appState)
```

That creates a SwiftUI view with:

```swift
@ObservedObject var appState: AppState
```

So when `mode` changes, SwiftUI has a direct dependency to invalidate the label view.

Use a fixed `NSStatusItem.length`. The width changes because `NSStatusItem.variableLength` sizes itself from the current button content, and your two SF Symbols have different intrinsic widths.

## Simple fix

When creating the status item, use a fixed length:

```swift
let statusItem = NSStatusBar.system.statusItem(withLength: NSStatusItem.squareLength)
self.statusItem = statusItem
```

or, if you already create it with `.variableLength`, set length after creation:

```swift
statusItem.length = NSStatusItem.squareLength
```

Then configure the button as image-only:

```swift
if let button = statusItem.button {
    button.imagePosition = .imageOnly
    button.imageScaling = .scaleProportionallyDown
}
```

Example:

```swift
@MainActor
private func setupMenuBar() {
    let statusItem = NSStatusBar.system.statusItem(withLength: NSStatusItem.squareLength)
    self.statusItem = statusItem

    if let button = statusItem.button {
        button.imagePosition = .imageOnly
        button.imageScaling = .scaleProportionallyDown
    }

    updateMenuBarIcon()

    let menu = NSMenu()
    menu.addItem(NSMenuItem(title: "Lock inputs", action: nil, keyEquivalent: ""))
    menu.addItem(NSMenuItem.separator())
    menu.addItem(
        NSMenuItem(
            title: "Quit",
            action: #selector(NSApplication.terminate(_:)),
            keyEquivalent: "q"
        )
    )
    statusItem.menu = menu
}
```

Then your toggle can keep doing:

```swift
@MainActor
private func updateMenuBarIcon() {
    let imageName = appState.mode == .telex
        ? "character.textbox.badge.sparkles"
        : "textformat.size.smaller"

    statusItem?.button?.image = NSImage(
        systemSymbolName: imageName,
        accessibilityDescription: "Lex"
    )
}
```

## If the glyph still visually shifts

A fixed `statusItem.length` keeps the status item slot fixed. But different SF Symbols can still look slightly off-center because their internal glyph bounds differ.

If that happens, also apply a fixed symbol point size:

```swift
@MainActor
private func updateMenuBarIcon() {
    let imageName = appState.mode == .telex
        ? "character.textbox.badge.sparkles"
        : "textformat.size.smaller"

    let image = NSImage(
        systemSymbolName: imageName,
        accessibilityDescription: "Lex"
    )

    let configuration = NSImage.SymbolConfiguration(pointSize: 16, weight: .regular)
    statusItem?.button?.image = image?.withSymbolConfiguration(configuration)
}
```

Recommended setup:

```swift
statusItem.length = NSStatusItem.squareLength
button.imagePosition = .imageOnly
button.imageScaling = .scaleProportionallyDown
```

That should stop the menu bar item width from changing and keep the icon centered in the same slot.

Replace this:

```swift
struct LexMenuBarLabel: View {
```

with this:

```swift
struct LexMenuBarLabel: View {
    @ObservedObject var appState: AppState

    var body: some View {
        ZStack {
            Image(systemName: "character.textbox.badge.sparkles")
                .opacity(appState.mode == .telex ? 1 : 0)

            Image(systemName: "character.textbox")
                .opacity(appState.mode == .literal ? 1 : 0)
        }
        .imageScale(.large)
        .frame(width: 24, height: 18, alignment: .center)
        .accessibilityLabel("Lex")
    }
}
```

Why this works:

- `ZStack` always contains both icons, so SwiftUI computes a stable intrinsic width from the largest one.
- `opacity(0)` hides the inactive icon but still keeps it in layout.
- `.frame(width: 24, height: 18)` gives the `MenuBarExtra` label a stable size.
- The visible icon changes, but the menu bar slot does not resize.

If `24` is too wide or narrow, tune only that value:

```swift

```

or:

```swift

```

Do not use conditional `Image(systemName:)` directly if you want fixed width:

```swift

```

`.frame(width:)` having no effect means `MenuBarExtra` is not honoring that SwiftUI frame as the status item’s measured width. With the declarative `MenuBarExtra` label, this can happen because macOS derives the menu bar item width from the rendered label/icon content, not from the SwiftUI frame modifier in the normal way.

Use a hidden “widest icon” as the layout reservation, then overlay the active icon. This stays declarative and keeps using `Label` / `systemImage` directly.

```swift
struct LexMenuBarLabel: View {
    @ObservedObject var appState: AppState

    var body: some View {
        Label("Lex", systemImage: "character.textbox.badge.sparkles")
            .labelStyle(.iconOnly)
            .opacity(0)
            .overlay {
                Label(
                    "Lex",
                    systemImage: appState.mode == .telex
                        ? "character.textbox.badge.sparkles"
                        : "character.textbox"
                )
                .labelStyle(.iconOnly)
            }
            .accessibilityLabel("Lex")
    }
}
```

Why this works better than `.frame(width:)`:

- The invisible base label uses the wider icon: `character.textbox.badge.sparkles`.
- `.opacity(0)` keeps it in layout, so the menu bar item reserves that width.
- `.overlay` draws the real active icon on top.
- The active icon can change without changing the reserved menu bar width.

If the active icon is not visually centered, add alignment to the overlay:

```swift
.overlay(alignment: .center) {
    Label(
        "Lex",
        systemImage: appState.mode == .telex
            ? "character.textbox.badge.sparkles"
            : "character.textbox"
    )
    .labelStyle(.iconOnly)
}
```

If even this does not affect the width, then SwiftUI `MenuBarExtra` is extracting the symbol in a way that bypasses normal layout. At that point, fixed menu bar width requires the AppKit path with `NSStatusItem.squareLength`; declarative `MenuBarExtra` does not expose an equivalent fixed-length API.
